patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH V10 00/10] famfs: port into fuse
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch aims to port the FAMFS file system into Fuse, but concerns have been raised about the use of a novel BPF ops structure for page table mappings.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the use of a novel BPF ops structure for page table mappings, citing potential performance issues and unfairness to users. Suggested refactoring the DAX layer to create an ops structure that could serve as a basis for the BPF solution.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Asked if MM people are on board with letting BPF handle page table mappings, seeking clarification on the feasibility of this approach.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: fix handling of NFSEXP_PNFS in the netlink codepath

version_history.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: fix handling of NFSEXP_PNFS in the netlink codepath

patches_submitted.patch_summary: The patch addresses a bug in the NFS server's handling of NFSEXP_PNFS in the netlink codepath, which was overlooked when reworking block layout checks. It ensures that pnfs is only enabled on xfs exports discovered via netlink if explicitly requested.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Applied the patch to nfsd-testing without issues, but noted that it will be folded into an existing series in his tree.
review_comments.raw_body:
From: Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>

On Tue, 14 Apr 2026 07:18:05 -0700, Jeff Layton wrote:
> The rework of how block layouts were checked moved the check for
> NFSEXP_PNFS out of nfsd4_setup_layout_type() and into the callers. That
> patch didn't account for the new call in nfsd4_setup_layout_type().
> 
> 

Applied to nfsd-testing, thanks!

For the moment Christoph's series is still in my tree.

[1/1] nfsd: fix handling of NFSEXP_PNFS in the netlink codepath
      commit: b8cefe5d302f351c252a833dd8c7e8f4a0cd5028

--
Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Confirmed that the patch fixes a bug and added a Reviewed-by tag.
review_comments.raw_body:
Looks good:

Reviewed-by: Christoph Hellwig <hch@lst.de>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 14/14] docs: fuse: add io-uring bufring and zero-copy documentation

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 14/14] docs: fuse: add io-uring bufring and zero-copy documentation

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 00/14] fuse: add io-uring buffer rings and zero-copy
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for buffer rings and zero-copy to the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) kernel module, utilizing io-uring capabilities. Buffer rings reduce memory usage by pooling payload buffers and selecting them on demand, while also laying the groundwork for pinned buffers that can be easily managed. Zero-copy is an opt-in feature for privileged servers that eliminates memory copies between the kernel and userspace for read/write-heavy operations. The patch series has been tested to show throughput improvements of up to 35% in certain scenarios.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about duplicated logic in the fuse_uring_send_next_to_ring() function, which contained duplicated logic from fuse_uring_send(). The author has simplified the logic by decoupling request fetching from the send operation and reducing unnecessary parameter passing. A new function fuse_uring_get_next_fuse_req() has been introduced to get the next fuse req, and the existing code has been refactored accordingly.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the copy_to_user() logic being scattered throughout the code, agreeing to centralize it in a new function called copy_header_to_ring(). The patch includes this new function and updates several places where copy_to_user() was used. A fix is planned for v2.
sentiment_signals: planned to address in future version

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the copy_from_user() logic in fuse_uring.c by moving it into a new function, copy_header_from_ring(), to centralize error handling and rate-limited logging.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the interface for copying headers in buffer rings, agreeing to use enum types to identify which part of the header needs to be copied, and proposed changes to simplify both kernel-space and user-space header addresses copying.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the import_ubuf() function being called multiple times in fuse_uring_copy_from_ring() and fuse_uring_args_to_ring(). They added a new helper function setup_fuse_copy_state() to contain the logic for setting up the copy state, which replaces these calls. The patch will need further revision.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about how to handle kaddr in fuse_copy_state, explaining that it's used for direct memcpy and adding a check to ensure len is non-zero before returning. The patch will need further revision.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about magic indices in the iovec array by replacing them with named constants FUSE_URING_IOV_HEADERS and FUSE_URING_IOV_PAYLOAD, making the code self-documenting and preparing for future buffer ring support.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about exposing implementation details in the header file by moving the fuse_uring_abort() function to dev_uring.c, where it belongs. The author agreed that this change will make subsequent commits cleaner and is a necessary step for adding pinning capabilities.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the placement of the fuse_uring_get_iovec_from_sqe() function in the file, agreeing to move it earlier for easier review of subsequent commits that add buffer rings.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the code is duplicating functionality from io_pin_pages() and suggested exporting it for use in this patch, also planning to apply a similar technique in ublk.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think this is a duplicate of io_pin_pages(), can we just export that
and use here? I'm basically going to propose to use the same technique
in ublk - would be another duplicate.


(Not a complete a review yet, just something I just noticed).


Thanks,
Bernd

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Bernd Schubert suggested modifying the fuse implementation to allow SQEs (Submission Queue Entries) with different iovecs for the payload buffer, enabling fuse-server to handle multiple IO sizes by specifying dedicated IO size per pBuf.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Joanne,

thanks a lot for this document! Could we discuss if we could just hook
in here and allow SQEs with different iovecs for the payload buffer?
Let's say fuse-server wants multiple IO sizes - it could easily do that
via different pBufs and just needs to specify the dedicated IO size per
pBuf. Those buffers could then get sorted into an array - we could
define either via FUSE init the number of buf sizes or use a fixed size
array. Fuse requests then would just need to pick the right array.
This is basically what I'm currently working on for ublk.

I think it would be good to agree on the design before it gets merged so
that uapi doesn't change.

Thanks,
Bernd

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author Joanne Koong is addressing a concern about the placement of io_pin_pages() logic, suggesting it should be moved to the mm layer as it's not specific to io-uring. She asks for clarification on whether the memory being passed in is user-allocated and not part of io-uring.
review_comments.raw_body:
Tbh I think this is generic logic that makes more sense to live in the
mm layer instead of fuse calling this as an exported io-uring
function. The memory it's passing in is not related to io-uring, so
that was my hesitation. For your ublk use case, is the memory you're
passing into this user-allocated memory that's not part of io-uring?
If so, then maybe it's best to move io_pin_pages() out of io-uring and
into generic mm.

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author responded to Bernd's feedback on fuse server preferring static preallocation over incremental buffer consumption by explaining their design choice for supporting multiple IO size payloads, which involves using a fixed-size header and an array of payload buffers with corresponding sizes.

Author is addressing a concern about the struct fuse_uring_cmd_req layout, specifically the placement of the buf_size field to accommodate multiple io size payloads. The author asks for clarification on how many different payload sizes are envisioned and seeks alignment with the reviewer's expectations.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: Gave Reviewed-by

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the #else directive in the patch was unnecessary and suggested moving the real function into the C file instead.
review_comments.raw_body:
I had it put in there because of

#else /* CONFIG_FUSE_IO_URING */

but sure, we can also move the real function into the c file.


Reviewed-by: Bernd Schubert <bernd@bsbernd.com>

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the original order of checks and allocation was intended, but is willing to accept the change if it's useful for future commits.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hmm, goal was to first do the checks and then to allocate, but if the
change is useful for future commits, fine with me.

Reviewed-by: Bernd Schubert <bernd@bsbernd.com>

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Bernd Schubert suggested renaming the variable 'queue_depth' to 'max_queue_depth', and also proposed dynamically registering ring entries when needed, which would make the current variable a maximum value rather than its actual value.

Reviewer noted that the patch does not properly handle the case where a fuse request is interrupted by an IORING_OP_READ_FIXED or IORING_OP_WRITE_FIXED operation, and requested additional error handling to prevent data corruption.
review_comments.raw_body:
Could we call this 'max_queue_depth'? I still think that it might be
useful to register ring entries dynamically when needed at some point.
And then this would become a 'max' value and not the actual value.

---

Thanks,
Bernd

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer questioned the necessity of registering a large number of buffer ring entries, suggesting that an entry is only useful when carrying data and implying that registering unnecessary entries may be inefficient.

Bernd Schubert noted that the current design allocates headers into a large region without utilizing extra space, but suggested using 4K allocations for headers to enable inlining small requests and making ring buffers useful by reducing metadata payload buffer allocation. He also mentioned that contiguous buffer allocation can be done for entries as well, but it becomes harder with dynamic entry registration.

Bernd Schubert noted that the use of pbuf for dynamic allocation in io-uring buffer rings is unnecessary, as libfuse can dynamically add or remove entries from the ring and their buffers without requiring a fixed number of SQEs/CQEs. He suggested adding a notification type to wake idle entries and removing them, which he believes would be an easy solution.

The reviewer suggested a design change to use per-queue buffer rings with an order map, which would allow the io-uring ring owner to have multiple pools for its own purposes and enable more efficient buffer recycling.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 01/14] fuse: separate next request fetching from sending logic
series_items.patch_summary: This patch separates the logic of fetching and sending the next request in the fuse kernel module, making the control flow clearer and reducing unnecessary parameter passing. The duplicated logic from fuse_uring_send() is removed, and a new function fuse_uring_get_next_fuse_req() is introduced to fetch the next request. The send operation is now handled by a separate function fuse_uring_send(). This change simplifies the code and improves maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 02/14] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying to ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic for copying headers to a ring in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring implementation. It extracts the header copying into a new function called copy_header_to_ring(), which makes the code more modular and easier to understand. The function handles errors and rate-limited logging, making it more robust. This change improves the maintainability of the code and reduces duplication.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 03/14] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying from ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic for copying headers from a ring in the fuse kernel module to make it more clear and centralized. It introduces a new function, copy_header_from_ring(), which handles the actual copying of data from user space to kernel space. This function includes error handling and rate-limited logging. The existing code is modified to use this new function instead of directly calling copy_from_user(). This change improves the maintainability and readability of the code by separating the header copying logic into a single, self-contained function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 04/14] fuse: use enum types for header copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the interface for copying headers in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation by introducing enum types to identify which part of the header needs to be copied. This change will simplify both kernel-space and user-space header addresses copying when buffer rings are added, making it easier to manage and maintain the code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 05/14] fuse: refactor setting up copy state for payload copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the code for setting up the copy state for payload copying in the FUSE file system. It introduces a new helper function `setup_fuse_copy_state()` that encapsulates the logic for importing user buffers and initializing the copy state. This simplifies the code and makes it easier to maintain by reducing duplication and improving modularity.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 06/14] fuse: support buffer copying for kernel addresses
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for direct buffer copying in fuse, allowing it to work with kernel addresses instead of requiring page mapping and unmapping. This is a preparatory step for supporting pinned buffers in fuse-over-io-uring. The patch modifies the fuse_copy_init, fuse_copy_finish, fuse_copy_fill, and fuse_copy_do functions to handle both user-space and kernel-space buffer copying. It introduces a new 'is_kaddr' flag in the fuse_copy_state structure to indicate whether the buffer is a kernel address or not.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 07/14] fuse: use named constants for io-uring iovec indices
series_items.patch_summary: This patch replaces magic indices in the io-uring iovec array with named constants, making the code self-documenting and preparing for future buffer ring support.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: Identified the need for named constants to replace magic indices, suggesting their use would improve code readability and prepare for future buffer ring support.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 08/14] fuse: move fuse_uring_abort() from header to dev_uring.c
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the fuse_uring_abort() function from an inline header definition to a C file, dev_uring.c, where it belongs due to its internal dependencies and implementation details.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: Suggested moving fuse_uring_abort() due to its internal dependencies and implementation details, but noted it was initially placed there for a specific configuration directive.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 09/14] fuse: rearrange io-uring iovec and ent allocation logic
series_items.patch_summary: This patch rearranges the io-uring iovec and ent allocation logic in fuse_uring.c to make it easier for future commits to add buffer rings. The changes involve moving the fuse_uring_get_iovec_from_sqe function earlier in the file and reordering the allocation logic in fuse_uring_create_ring_ent.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: Expressed some reservations about the change, but was fine with it if it's useful for future commits. Did not provide any specific technical concerns or suggestions.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 10/14] fuse: add io-uring buffer rings
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for buffer rings to the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring interface, allowing servers to communicate more efficiently by reducing memory usage and laying the groundwork for future optimizations.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Bernd Schubert suggested renaming queue_depth to max_queue_depth, and expressed interest in dynamically registering ring entries at some point, which would make queue_depth a 'max' value rather than the actual value.

reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where the payload buffer is too small to accommodate all entries, and requested a check for this condition
review_comments.raw_body:
Could we call this 'max_queue_depth'? I still think that it might be
useful to register ring entries dynamically when needed at some point.
And then this would become a 'max' value and not the actual value.

---

Thanks,
Bernd

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 11/14] fuse: add pinned headers capability for io-uring buffer rings
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the capability to pin header buffers for io-uring buffer rings in fuse servers. When enabled, the kernel pins the header pages and uses direct memcpy for copying, avoiding per-request overhead of pinning and unpinning user pages. The pinned pages are accounted against RLIMIT_MEMLOCK and tracked using mm->pinned_vm. Unpinning is done during connection abort in process context.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Bernd Schubert suggested using the existing io_pin_pages() function instead of duplicating its functionality in this patch, and mentioned that a similar technique could be applied to another project (ublk) as well.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think this is a duplicate of io_pin_pages(), can we just export that
and use here? I'm basically going to propose to use the same technique
in ublk - would be another duplicate.


(Not a complete a review yet, just something I just noticed).


Thanks,
Bernd

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author agrees that the logic for pinning memory should be moved to the mm layer, not in an exported io-uring function, and asks for clarification on whether the user-allocated memory is related to io-uring.
review_comments.raw_body:
Tbh I think this is generic logic that makes more sense to live in the
mm layer instead of fuse calling this as an exported io-uring
function. The memory it's passing in is not related to io-uring, so
that was my hesitation. For your ublk use case, is the memory you're
passing into this user-allocated memory that's not part of io-uring?
If so, then maybe it's best to move io_pin_pages() out of io-uring and
into generic mm.

Thanks,
Joanne

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 12/14] fuse: add pinned payload buffers capability for io-uring buffer rings
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the capability to pin payload buffers in io-uring buffer rings, allowing for direct memcpy of large payload copies and reducing overhead. The pinned pages are accounted against RLIMIT_MEMLOCK and unpinned during connection abort. This feature is particularly beneficial for high-performance systems and can result in a 10% throughput improvement for direct randreads and other operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 13/14] fuse: add zero-copy over io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for zero-copy data transfer over io-uring in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) kernel module, eliminating unnecessary memory copies between userspace, the kernel, and the fuse server for page-backed read/write operations. The feature requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges and buffer rings with pinned headers and buffers. When enabled, the kernel registers client pages as a sparse buffer at the entry's fixed id via io_buffer_register_bvec(), allowing the fuse server to perform direct io-uring read/write operations on these pages. Non-page-backed arguments still go through the payload buffer as normal. The patch also includes details on the request flow for zero-copy write operations.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) module, specifically covering its usage of io-uring buffer rings and zero-copy techniques. The documentation explains how FUSE uses buffer rings to reduce memory usage and enable pinned buffers, as well as the flow of requests through these buffer rings. This patch aims to improve understanding and usability of FUSE with io-uring by providing clear and detailed documentation on its internal workings.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Bernd Schubert suggested allowing SQEs with different iovecs for the payload buffer, enabling fuse-server to handle multiple IO sizes by specifying dedicated IO size per pBuf, and proposed defining a fixed-size array or using FUSE init to determine the number of buf sizes.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Joanne,

thanks a lot for this document! Could we discuss if we could just hook
in here and allow SQEs with different iovecs for the payload buffer?
Let's say fuse-server wants multiple IO sizes - it could easily do that
via different pBufs and just needs to specify the dedicated IO size per
pBuf. Those buffers could then get sorted into an array - we could
define either via FUSE init the number of buf sizes or use a fixed size
array. Fuse requests then would just need to pick the right array.
This is basically what I'm currently working on for ublk.

I think it would be good to agree on the design before it gets merged so
that uapi doesn't change.

Thanks,
Bernd

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author responded to Bernd's feedback about fuse server preferring static preallocation over incremental buffer consumption by explaining their design choice for supporting multiple IO size payloads, which involves using an array of buf_sizes in the uapi and separate ring buffers for each pool.

The author is addressing a concern about the struct fuse_uring_cmd_req layout, specifically that the buf_size field should be last to accommodate multiple io size payloads. The author asks for clarification on how many different payload sizes are envisioned and requests alignment with the reviewer's intention.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer questioned the necessity of registering a large number of buffer ring entries, pointing out that an empty entry is useless and suggesting reducing the number of registered entries.

The reviewer noted that the current design maps headers into a large region and does not utilize extra space, but suggested using 4K allocations for headers to enable inlining of small requests and making ring buffers more useful.

Reviewer Bernd Schubert questioned the necessity of using pbuf for dynamic allocation in io-uring, citing that there is no technical reason to do so and suggesting alternative approaches such as adding a notification type like FUSE_NOTIFY_WAKE_RING_ENTRIES to remove idle entries.

The reviewer proposed an alternative implementation of fuse_bufring where each queue has a separate struct, including an order map and a dynamically created pool lookup table, to improve performance and flexibility.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH V10 00/10] famfs: port into fuse
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch series ports the famfs file system into fuse, allowing for a generic mapping interface that can be used by multiple servers. The alternative approach involves using bpf maps to find and compute extent mappings, which is minimal and already written. This approach would require renaming functions in fs/fuse/famfs.c to generic names and moving daxdev setup to connection initialization time. The main concerns are whether this approach introduces technical limitations on famfs's production workloads, such as performance drops or feasibility issues with high-scale systems.

review_comments.author: Darrick Wong
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that the patch's use of a bpf approach would eliminate the need for a famfs-specific mapping interface, which aligns with Miklos' statement, and suggested reevaluating the alternative approach.

Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that using struct fuse_iomap_io objects from a user's BPF program would be inconsistent with the original purpose and design of this data structure, which originated in the fuse-iomap patchset.

Reviewer Darrick Wong expressed concern that the famfs code would be permanently tied to fuse, even if native non-bpf support for famfs is added later, and suggested exploring an alternative approach that uses bpf maps to make the famfs uapi additions generic.

Reviewer noted that the custom hashtable map implementation in kernel/bpf/hashtab.c lacks self-rehashing, which is a limitation compared to rhashtable's ability to reduce bucket list chasing.

Reviewer Darrick Wong expressed concern that the famfs code in this series cannot be removed from fuse if it lands, as fuse will need to support it forever due to its native non-bpf support for famfs. He suggested verifying whether the alternative approach using bpf maps would introduce technical limitations on production workloads.

Reviewer Darrick Wong expressed concern that the patch would make it difficult to remove famfs code from fuse in the future, and suggested verifying whether using bpf maps leads to performance issues on production workloads.

Reviewer Darrick Wong expressed concern that the patch does not make the famfs uapi additions to fuse generic enough, potentially leading to irreversible changes if the alternative approach is not taken.

Reviewer Darrick Wong expressed concerns that the use of BPF in this patch is not about technical issues, but rather about avoiding slow or unresponsive maintainers and the kernel community's reluctance to explore alternative paths for building new user APIs.
review_comments.raw_body:
<nod> On second thought, there's a way to read Miklos' sentence that I
hadn't thought of before:

"However, with the [fuse-iomap] bpf approach, this [famfs specific
mapping interface] would be gone, which is great."

vs. the way I had thought:

"However, with the bpf approach, this [famfs specific mapping interface]
would be gone [in favor of filling out a struct iomap directly], which
is great."

So maybe Miklos actually /has/ at least read all the way through the
February posting, though I have no data to make such a conclusion. :/

---

Remember where struct fuse_iomap_io came from -- the fuse-iomap
patchset.  It would be rather odd to start accepting fuse_iomap_io
objects from a user's bpf program without examining the rest of the fuse
iomap stuff.

---

I agree that you and I and John could probably get the code and review
part wrapped up in perhaps two merge windows -- one for fuse-iomap,
and the second for famfs.  The userspace parts of both are more or less
done, which would minimize the amount of rework when we get to the
libfuse part.

(Let's be honest, with LSFMM happening during the week between -rc2 and
-rc3 and everyone's travel thereto, that's going to blow a big hole in
the 7.2 schedule)

The question is, would Miklos acquiesce to merging a large ball of code
that the three of us have been collaborating on?  Even if he wasn't
deeply involved in that collaboration?

---

I see a custom hashtable map implementation in kernel/bpf/hashtab.c,
and no particular evidence that it can rehash itself to cut down on
bucket list chasing.  That's too bad, because rhashtable rehashing is
generally effective at keeping the xfs icache pointer chasing down.

If we have a per-inode famfs_file_meta object, I wonder if we could just
attach it to the fuse_inode as a void *private pointer?  That wouldn't
be any worse than current famfs.

---

I've asked that question (are we going to have millions of mappings?)
before.  From what John has told me and what I've seen with cxl and pmem
devices before that, the memory manager is heavily incentivized to give
out large static(ish) allocations to constrain the metadata overhead,
enable the use of PMD/PGD TLB entries, and minimize pointer chasing
through mapping structures.

The only reason we let that happen in the disk filesystems is that the
IO service times are so high nobody cares about L3 misses.

---

I think John's been pretty clear that he doesn't want to drag this out
even a day longer.  Given current trends this month, I might run out of
time soon too.

---

(Oh good, nobody's talking about going the evil route and just fill out
struct iomap directly!)

---

Well ok, here we go -- I don't think there's any serious technical
problems with BPF.  The ability to read (and in some cases write) to
kernel memory looks like it's flexible enough to do the classification
and data collection stuff that most current bpf users want to do.

The issues I was alluding to are BPF being used as a means to get around
slow/unresponsive maintainers; and the kernel community's collective
refusal to explore any other path to building new user APIs besides
designing everything generically perfectly up front in the kernel UABI
along with all the stress that involves.

Once upon a time I tried to push on these UAPI stressfulness issues and
Linus told me I had a loose grip on reality.  He's probably right.

review_comments.author: John Groves
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer questioned the feasibility of making the mapping interface generic, citing concerns that it may not be realistic to achieve this goal.

Reviewer John Groves disputed the original patch's premise that famfs-specific code must be merged into fuse, pointing out that the FUSE_DAX_FMAP capability can be used to advertise or not advertise the GET_FMAP and GET_DAXDEV commands, allowing for a future switch to BPF without modifying existing code.

Reviewer pointed out that advertised capabilities allow for contraction of UABI-handling code, enabling removal of unreachable code

Reviewer John Groves expressed concern that using BPF for the vma fault handler in famfs may be too performance-critical and potentially impact production workloads, suggesting further discussion with experts to determine feasibility.

Reviewer John Groves expressed concerns that the famfs code in this series cannot be removed from fuse if it lands, as it would always need to have native non-bpf support for famfs, and requested further investigation into technical limitations on production workloads.
review_comments.raw_body:
Mapping interfaces being generic is a nice idea, but I'm no sure it's
realistic in a generalized sense. But other mitigating comments below.

---

I believe this basic premise is completely wrong. Here is why:

There is a FUSE_DAX_FMAP capability that the kernel may advertise or not
at init time; this capability "is" the famfs GET_FMAP AND GET_DAXDEV 
commands. In the future, if we find a way to use BPF (or some other 
mechanism) to avoid needing those fuse messages, the kernel could be updated 
to NEVER advertise the FUSE_DAX_FMAP capability. All of the famfs-specific 
code could be taken out of kernels that never advertise that capability.

Simple, really. Can't re-use the message opcodes, but as Darrick pointed out
those are not a scarce resource.

---

Um, advertised capabilities allow contraction of the UABI-handling code with 
only some small cruft. Code that is only reachable in the presence of dead 
capability can totally be removed.

---

See my long comment to Darrick's prior email.

I suspect that this would be the only place BPF has been tried for a vma
fault handler. That is a special, performance critical path - especially
for famfs. In discussion with the right people we can probably reason
through whether this is a non-starter or not.

---

Regards,
John

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: fuse/io-uring: Proposal to support pBuf in additon to kBuf
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch proposes to support pBuf in addition to kBuf for the fuse and io-uring interfaces. The change comes from a revised approach where kernel-managed buffers are no longer an integral part of the io-uring infrastructure, but rather self-contained within the fuse implementation. This design allows for user-allocated buffers to be passed during registration time, with optional pinning for optimization. Zero-copy requires both header and payload buffer pinning, which is subject to CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges. The patch addresses concerns about mlock requirements and aims to maintain compatibility with existing fuse and io-uring contracts.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Bernd Schubert noted that he thought Jens had already accepted the kBuf changes and was unaware of the change from v1 to v2, which dropped the use of kBuf in favor of user-allocated buffers.
review_comments.raw_body:
yes absolutely it does. I had actually thought that Jens had already
accepted the kBuf changes. I had seen the discussion with Pavel, but
then seen (at least I think) that Jens had accepted it. And with the
different versions, I didn't notice that v2 (in my counting that is v5,
I think), doesn't use kBuf anymore.

If I understand it right, the io-uring bvec changes are only needed for
zero-copy?

Thanks,
Bernd

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the patch was previously merged but later dropped, providing no further explanation or plan for reintegration.

Author confirms that the issue of mlock requirement has been addressed in v2 by dropping kernel-managed buffers as an io-uring native infrastructure and instead allowing user allocations during registration time.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yes it had been briefly merged into Jens's io_uring/for-7.1 staging
tree and then it was dropped.

---

Yes, that is correct.

Thanks,
Joanne

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/percpu, memcontrol: Per-memcg-lruvec percpu accounting

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/percpu, memcontrol: Per-memcg-lruvec percpu accounting

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's memory accounting system to provide more detailed information about per-memcg-lruvec (per-cgroup, per-node, and per-LRU vector) percpu allocations. It does this by converting a certain type of memory tracking from a per-memcg level to a per-node level, allowing for better visibility into memory pressure on multi-NUMA systems. The patch also introduces separate accounting for node-level statistics and memcg-lruvec statistics, which requires exposing a new function to be used outside of the memory control module. This change improves the kernel's ability to track memory usage at a finer granularity, particularly for percpu allocations, but has a small memory overhead and negligible performance impact.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a mistake in exporting mod_memcg_lruvec_state, which was unnecessary for this patch and only leftover from another series. The author apologized for the 'noise' and promised no further changes are needed.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello Matthew, thank you for reviewing my patch!

Whoops, no module uses this symbol in this patch. I was working on a similar
patch series (accounting zswap statistics on a per-lruvec level [1]) where I did
the same thing, and needed to export this so that using it in zsmalloc
wouldn't break if zram was built as a module.

Obviously there's no need for exporting for this patch but I forgot to drop
that part when copying this over from that series. Sorry for the noise!

Thanks again for catching this. I hope you have a great day!
Joshua

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260311195153.4013476-10-joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com/

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the patch may introduce some inaccuracy by charging based on possible CPUs, but considers it acceptable and plans to address this in v3.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hey Harry, thanks for giving this patch a look!

Yes, definitely. I think the solution is just to charge based on possible
CPUs, even if that might lead to some inaccuracy (by however many CPUs
aren't online at that moment). Seems like that's what already happens
in memcg anyways, so I think this discrepancy is OK to tolerate.

Will spin up a v3! Thanks a lot, Harry! Have a great day : -)
Joshua

review_comments.author: Harry (Oracle)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Harry noted a potential issue with the patch, specifically questioning whether there is an equivalent function for getting the number of possible CPUs on a node.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yeah, that sounds fine to me.
Not sure if there's nr_cpus_node() equivalent for possible CPUs though.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges a technical challenge in accurately tracking physical locations of pcpu allocations that fall back to another node, and is seeking input on whether the misattribution is acceptable or if iterating through all pages at allocation/free time is necessary.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello reviewers,

Since I submitted this, I have been thinking about the feedback that Sashiko
has given this patch [1]. Harry has already pointed out the points about
drifting due to CPU hotplug, but one there is one particular concern that
I have been trying to tackle with no avail.

The issue is, pcpu allocations for CPUs on node A may actually fall back to
node B, if node A is out of space and under pressure. This design seems to be
intentional, to prevent memory pressure from failing these allocations.

However, this means that we cannot charge percpu memory based on the number
of CPUs present on a node, because although the memory "belongs" to the node
(since the CPU it actually belongs to is on the node), the memory can be
serviced from elsewhere.

To handle this, I've tried several approaches. All of them were either too
expensive (iterating through all pages at allocation / free time) or introduces
new drift (I thought of managing per-chunk statistics as well).

To be honest, I think I'm out of ideas at this point :/ So I wanted to see
what others thought about how to track physical locations for pcpu allocations
that were allocated via fallback. Are these rare enough that we are OK with
the misattributing here? Should we eat the cost of iterating through all pages
to find out where it is physically?

Or is this patch not worth pursuing at the moment? ; -)

I hope this all makes sense. Thank you all in advance!
Joshua

[1] https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260404033844.1892595-1-joshua.hahnjy%40gmail.com

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v2] mm/percpu, memcontrol: Per-memcg-lruvec percpu accounting
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch addresses an issue where per-memcg-lruvec percpu accounting is inaccurate due to CPU hotplug and memory pressure causing pcpu allocations to fall back to another node. The problem arises because the allocated memory can be serviced from a different node, making it difficult to charge percpu memory based on the number of CPUs present on a node. The patch attempts to tackle this issue by exploring various approaches, but none have been successful so far.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 3/4] mm/vmstat: remove unused __node_stat_* wrappers
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch removes unused wrapper functions __node_stat_* in mm/vmstat, which were used to modify node statistics. The removal is suggested by the original author, but Joshua Hahn notes that there are differences between the wrapped and unwrapped versions of one function, potentially introducing additional overhead.

review_comments.author: Ye Liu
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Ye Liu noted that the removal of __node_stat_* wrappers does not introduce any functional differences in terms of atomicity or overhead, as both functions ultimately use __mod_node_page_state and mod_node_page_state is defined as __mod_node_page_state.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thank you for your review, Joshua. Regarding the difference between
__node_stat_mod_folio and node_stat_mod_folio: in the current implementation,
both functions ultimately use __mod_node_page_state, as mod_node_page_state
is defined as __mod_node_page_state. There is no functional difference
between them in terms of atomicity or overhead. The __ prefixed versions
were wrappers that are now unused, which is why we're removing them.

-- 
Thanks,
Ye Liu

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: Author disputed Ye's claim that the patch introduces additional overhead from the cmpxchg loop, pointing out that mod_node_page_state calls mod_node_state which includes the cmpxchg loop, and suggested documenting this effect instead.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello Ye,

Thanks for the quick response. However, I'm not sure that's true.
For #ifdef CONFIG_HAVE_CMPXCHG_LOCAL in mm/vmstat.c, I can see that
mod_node_page_state calls mod_node_state, which includes the cmpxchg loop.
I think many users enalbe this config, including x86. IMHO, we should document
this effect of the changes.

Thank you, I hope you have a great day!
Joshua

review_comments.author: Ye Liu
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that removing __node_stat_* wrappers changes the update path from direct per-cpu diff update to a serializing update path, incurring extra overhead due to cmpxchg loop or local_irq_save/restore on certain systems.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thank you for your review, Joshua. You are correct that there is a difference.
The __node_stat_mod_folio call goes directly to __mod_node_page_state, which
assumes the caller already has the necessary serialization. By contrast,
node_stat_mod_folio goes through mod_node_page_state, which on systems with
CONFIG_HAVE_CMPXCHG_LOCAL uses a this_cpu_try_cmpxchg() loop and on other
systems serializes via local_irq_save/restore.

That means the wrapper removal is not purely cosmetic: it changes the
update path from a direct per-cpu diff update to a serializing update
path, and therefore incurs extra overhead.

-- 
Thanks,
Ye Liu

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [RFC, PATCH 00/12] userfaultfd: working set tracking for VM guest memory
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch introduces working set tracking for VM guest memory using the userfaultfd mechanism, allowing for serialization of reclaim against memory accesses and enabling the decision on where to place cold pages from userspace. The approach involves flipping a single bit under the mmap lock to switch between tracking and reclaiming, ensuring that read-touched pages are not swapped out. This patch is part of a larger series (PATCH 00/12) and aims to improve memory management in VM guests.

review_comments.author: Peter Xu
review_comments.summary:
reviewer noted that the patch has little to do with userfaultfd and suggested that its relevance would become apparent upon closer inspection

reviewer expressed enthusiasm for the patch and suggested discussing its details on the list or elsewhere, noting that it will help determine the most acceptable path forward

Reviewer Peter Xu noted that the proposed solution for tracking hot pages still relies on access bit information already handled by mm core, and suggested separating this problem from the existing one or reporting hotness directly from kernel using a READ interface.

Reviewer Peter Xu noted that using RW protection instead of WR-ONLY protection would only slightly differ if a page gets read-only during evicting, and suggested allowing a small window for refaults to minimize false positives.

reviewer questioned the benefit of avoiding an extra TLB flush when evicting, suggesting that some numbers would be necessary to justify this optimization and also asked a question about how management applications can read pages protected by PROT_NONE
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks.  Note that the proposal doesn't have much with userfaultfd.  You'll
see when you start reading.

---

I won't be there (as it's rejected.. hence not invited).  But I'm always
happy to discuss on this topic on the list or elsewhere.  Alone the way I
believe it'll also help us to know what is the most acceptable path
forward as it's still very relevant.

---

Yes, I get your point.

It's just that it'll still partly done what access bit has already been
doing for mm core in general on tracking hotness.  So I wonder if we should
still try to see if we can separate the two problems.

One other quick thought is maybe we could also report hotness from kernel
directly rather than relying on async faults, you can refer to "(2) Hotness
Information API" in my above proposal.  Here when it's only about knowing
which page is less frequently used, it's only a READ interface.

---

This is a good point.

When it's considered on top of your above "async trapping to collect
hotness with userfaultfd" idea, it flows naturally with this idea indeed.

However, IMHO that should really be an extremely small window, and the
major part the userapp should rely on is the larger window sampling
whether, in your current case, PROT_NONE (or PTE_NONE for shmem) switched
back to a accessable PTE.

It means using RW protection v.s. WR-ONLY protection will only differ very
slightly if by accident some page got read-only during evicting.  For
example, if the mgmt app monitors PROT_NONE state for 30 seconds, make a
decision to evict, evicting takes 5ms, then within 5ms someone read the
page.  It means it only misses the 5ms/30sec access pattern of guest.

So far I don't yet know if this would justify a new kernel API just for
that small false postive reporting some page is cold but actually it's hot.
To me it's still fine to consider using WP-ONLY and just allow that trivial
window to get refaulted later, because it shouldn't be the majority.

---

This is indeed a concern, maybe a bigger one.  I don't know how much
benefit we can get from avoiding one extra TLB flush when evicting.  IMHO
some numbers might be more than great to justify this part.

While at this, I do have a pure question that is relevant on the full
protection scheme (and it can be naive; please bare with me on not yet
reading the whole series): if you change anon mappings to PROT_NONE in
pgtables, then how do the mgmt app reads this page before dumping it to
anywhere? It's not like shmem where you can have a separate mapping.

Do you need to fork(), for example?

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3] btrfs: add BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS ioctl
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new unprivileged ioctl, BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS, which allows userspace to query the on-disk checksums for a file without exposing sensitive information about inaccessible files. The ioctl takes a struct btrfs_ioctl_get_csums_args with offset and length details, and writes a struct btrfs_ioctl_get_csums_entry into a provided buffer along with the checksums if available. This is primarily used to speed up mkfs.btrfs --rootdir by avoiding recalculating checksums for files that already have them stored on disk.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Qu Wenruo suggested that btrfs_ioctl_get_csums_entry::type should be bit flags instead of a single value, allowing for easier expansion in the future to return csums for encrypted or compressed data.
review_comments.raw_body:
Looks good to me for the current usage.

Reviewed-by: Qu Wenruo <wqu@suse.com>


Although the discussion about encryption makes me wonder if 
btrfs_ioctl_get_csums_entry::type should be bit flags.

For now it's fine, but as Daniel mentioned, in the future we may want to 
return the csum of such encrypted/compressed data.
In that case we need extra checks on the @flags to determine if 
ENCRYPTED/COMPRESSED is followed by a csum or not.

If @type is bit flags, we can easily have things like 
COMPRESSED|HAS_CSUM to indicate that future change.

Feel free to ignore this, it's just making the future expansion easier, 
not critical and we can definitely find a way using the new @flags to 
handle future change.

Thanks,
Qu

review_comments.author: David Sterba
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the new ioctl is similar to an existing proposed ioctl for deduplication and suggested it could be a wrapper for the search tree ioctl on the checksum tree, rather than a standalone implementation.

Reviewer noted that repeated mkfs operations are not typical, but acknowledged the significant time savings for preparing images; also mentioned potential use by deduplication tools and required security pass on final version

Reviewer David Sterba suggested clearing the partial range globally at the start of the ioctl, regardless of how many data is returned, but noted that this could have a performance hit for large buffers and repeated calls.

Reviewer David Sterba noted that the ioctl is not interruptible, which would cause issues if the operation is interrupted while holding the inode lock; he suggested adding an inode lock helper or calling down_rwsem_interruptible() to address this.

Reviewer David Sterba requested initialization of a buffer with zeros using = { 0 } instead of relying on memset() for partially filled buffers, and also suggested checking the final patch for unnecessary line breaks that exceed 80 characters.
review_comments.raw_body:
In the past reading the checksums was desired for deduplication, and we
had some proposed ioctls but I think it was not extensive to also cover
the holes, compression. Basically a wrapper for the search tree ioctl on
the checksum tree.

Handling all the types in this patch seems to be complete.

---

Repeated mkfs is a specific use case, normaly it happens just once but I
understand that for preparing various images the time savings are
significiant.

Once this ioctl is available I think the deduplication tools will make
use of it as an initial filter before actually doing the real
deduplication of extents.

As this is a unprivileged ioctl and touching structures and memory we'll
need a security pass on the final version. So far I haven't see any
issues known to me.

---

Clearning the partial range here is good but I think it should be done
globally right at the start of the ioctl, regardless of how many data we
return.

OTOH this can have a peformance hit in case a large buffer is provided
by default but for various ranges and possibly short ones, not matching
the entire buffer. So repeated calls will have to clear 16M but return
a few kilobytes.

---

This needs to be interruptible, all other points are. There's no inode
lock helper for that so you'd have to add it or call
down_rwsem_interruptible().

---

Please initialize that right away with = { 0 }, it's done later by
memset() but for something filled partially it's better to do it at the
beginning than forgetting to do it later.

Overall comment, please check the final patch that there are not
unnecessary line breaks that overflow 80 chars only slightly. I've seen
a few and would have fixed it but I think I've been pointing that out
enough in the past. I'll fix anything that's left if needed.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v6 24/43] btrfs: add extent encryption context tree item type
Re: [PATCH v3] btrfs: add BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS ioctl
Re: [PATCH v5 00/21] Virtual Swap Space
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Nhat Pham analyzed the performance of Kairui's Virtual Swap Space patches and identified areas for improvement, specifically xarray lookups in the physical swap allocator.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: Provided detailed analysis of performance issues in Kairui's Virtual Swap Space patches, specifically xarray lookups in the physical swap allocator. Suggested improvements to address these issues.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Apr 14, 2026 at 10:23 AM Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> * I still think there's a good chance we can *significantly* close the
> gap overall between a design with virtual swap and a design without.
> It's a bit premature to commit to a vswap-optional route (which to be
> completely honest I'm still not confident is possible to satisfy all
> of our requirements).

And to further note - these benchmark measure, in effect, purely swap
overhead. In a production environment with a lot of non-swap work, as
long as the gap is close enough I think we would be fine, even for a
hostile case like a fast swapfile-backend (I assume SSD swap's
bottleneck will be the IO mostly).

I will stare at your responses to see if there is other benchmark I
can play with, but it would be very helpful if you can share your full
suite :)

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [syzbot] [mm?] [cgroups?] WARNING: bad unlock balance in lruvec_stat_mod_folio
